title: Add prediction mode — 'what happens next?' before each step across all protocols
description: The single highest-impact practice feature. Before each step advances, pause the visualization and present a multiple-choice question derived from the next event's properties. The engine already computes the answer — prediction is purely a UI layer. Research: testing effect (Roediger & Karpicke 2006) shows students who test themselves learn 50% more than those who re-study. Desirable difficulty research confirms prediction before reveal strengthens encoding. Design 27 prediction questions (3 per protocol) covering TCP sequence numbers, TLS encryption states, DNS hierarchy, CORS preflight decisions, CDN cache logic, and more.
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction mode toggle available on every protocol visualization
Before each step, a multiple-choice question appears derived from the next event
Correct answer shown with reinforcement explanation after user commits
Wrong answer shown with visual feedback using the visualization itself
At least 3 prediction questions per protocol (27+ total)
Questions auto-generated from engine event data where possible

title: Add manual tracing mode — user IS the protocol (TCP server, DNS resolver, browser CORS, CDN edge)
description: Transform passive watching into active participation. 4 exercises: (1) 'You Are the TCP Server' — user constructs SYN-ACK with correct SEQ/ACK numbers, (2) 'You Are the DNS Resolver' — user clicks which server to query next in the hierarchy, (3) 'You Are the Browser (CORS)' — user decides preflight/direct/block for given fetch() config, (4) 'You Are the CDN Edge POP' — user applies Cache-Control/SWR rules to decide hit/miss/stale. Research: Cisco Packet Tracer achieves 95.2% skill acquisition via constructive learning. Active recall is more effective than recognition (Osmosis 2024).
acceptanceCriteria: TCP tracing: user selects flags and enters SEQ/ACK numbers, validated against engine
DNS tracing: user clicks server to query, with wrong-answer feedback explaining hierarchy
CORS tracing: user decides preflight/direct/block for multi-step CORS flow
CDN tracing: user applies cache rules to decide serve/fetch/stale actions
Each exercise provides detailed feedback on wrong answers with visual explanation

title: Add 10 scenario-based protocol selection challenges (interview prep)
description: Present real-world scenarios where the user must choose the right protocol and explain why. Covers: real-time chat (WebSocket vs SSE), mobile API (REST vs GraphQL), game state (TCP vs UDP), microservice RPC (REST vs gRPC), network switching (HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3), CDN invalidation, CORS configuration, serialization for Kafka, IoT messaging, multi-platform API. Each scenario has correct answer, explanation, most common wrong answer with why it's wrong. Directly maps to system design interview questions.
acceptanceCriteria: 10 scenario questions with protocol selection options
Each has a correct answer with detailed explanation
Each has a 'most common wrong answer' with explanation of misconception
Scoring tracks percentage correct per scenario category
Accessible from a 'Practice' tab or mode

title: Add 5 debugging challenges — find the bug in a protocol configuration
description: Show broken configurations and ask the user to identify the bug: (1) CORS credentials+wildcard conflict, (2) TCP CLOSE_WAIT stuck — server never sends FIN, (3) DNS TTL=0 on CNAME — stale cache misunderstanding, (4) HTTP/2 server push rejected — browser already has cached version, (5) CDN cache purge failure — Vary:Accept-Encoding creates separate cache entries. Each challenge shows the broken state, asks for diagnosis, provides visual feedback using the module's own visualizations.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 debugging challenges with interactive diagnosis interface
Each shows the symptom (error message, stuck state, stale data)
User must identify root cause from multiple-choice options
Feedback shows the fix with animated visualization of correct behavior
Challenges reference real-world debugging scenarios

title: Add 15-question progressive difficulty ladder (Bloom's Levels 1-5)
description: Structured question set across 5 Bloom's levels: Level 1 RECALL (3 questions: SYN meaning, TLS RTT count, DNS hierarchy order), Level 2 UNDERSTAND (3: why TLS 1.3 fewer RTTs, HTTP/2 HOL, WS masking purpose), Level 3 APPLY (3: CORS preflight prediction, CDN SWR calculation, TCP ACK arithmetic), Level 4 ANALYZE (3: HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 under packet loss, REST vs GraphQL for public API, DNS CNAME zone crossing), Level 5 EVALUATE/CREATE (3: design CORS headers for 3 origins, evaluate CDN caching strategy, design protocol architecture for multi-component system).
acceptanceCriteria: 15 questions organized into 5 difficulty levels
Each level requires 80% on the previous level to unlock
Question formats vary: free text, multiple choice, drag-and-drop, interactive builder
Answers evaluated with detailed feedback at every level
Progress indicator shows current Bloom's level reached

title: Add wrong-answer feedback system with visual explanations using existing visualizations
description: Practice without feedback is useless. When a user answers wrong: (1) don't just say 'wrong' — show WHY using the visualization, (2) advance the animation and highlight where the prediction diverged, (3) provide 3-level hints (rephrase, clue, show first step). Research: Brilliant.org's real-time mistake-specific feedback catches misconceptions immediately. Khan Academy's skill-level-change card shows trajectory. Design: TCP wrong ACK → show byte ruler, CORS wrong preflight → show decision tree with highlight on the triggering rule, DNS wrong server → show hierarchy with arrow to correct server.
acceptanceCriteria: Every wrong answer gets a specific explanation of WHY it's wrong
Explanation uses the visualization itself (highlight, animate, annotate)
3-level hint system: rephrase → clue → show first step
Common misconceptions identified and addressed specifically
Feedback varies based on mistake type (confusion pair, off-by-one, wrong mental model)

title: Add per-protocol mastery scoring (0-5 stars) with Khan Academy-style progression
description: Each of 11 protocol topics gets an independent 0-5 star mastery rating. Star requirements escalate: 1★=attempted, 2★=70% on recall+understand, 3★=80% on apply + 2 manual tracings, 4★=80% on analyze + 1 debugging, 5★=80% on evaluate/create + maintained across 3 spaced sessions. Decay mechanic: stars 3-5 decay one level after 14 days without practice (implements spaced repetition incentive). Visual: stars shown as gray→bronze→silver→gold→emerald progression on sidebar protocol cards.
acceptanceCriteria: Each protocol shows mastery stars in the sidebar
Stars progress based on question difficulty and accuracy
Star 5 requires correct answers across multiple sessions (not one sitting)
Stars 3-5 decay after 14 days without practice
Visual progression: gray→bronze→silver→gold→emerald

title: Add streak mechanics and XP system for practice engagement
description: Prediction streak: track consecutive correct predictions within a session. Display flame icon with count. Thresholds: 3=encouragement, 5=1.5x XP bonus, 10=2x XP. Daily practice streak: calendar showing consecutive days with practice. Milestones unlock bonus content (7 days → TCP Edge Cases, 14 days → HTTP/3 QUIC Deep Dive). Streak freeze: earned after 7-day streak, preserves streak for 1 missed day. Research: Duolingo's streak mechanics make users 3x more likely to return daily; apps with streak+milestone mechanics reduce 30-day churn by 35% (Forrester 2024).
acceptanceCriteria: Prediction streak counter visible during practice
XP awarded for correct answers with streak multiplier at 5+ and 10+
Daily streak shown as calendar visualization
Milestone unlocks at 3, 7, 14, 30, 60, 100 days
Streak freeze mechanic earned and consumed correctly

title: Add session summary with performance breakdown and weakness identification
description: End-of-session panel showing: protocols practiced with star changes, questions attempted with accuracy breakdown by Bloom's level and question type, best prediction streak, top strength and needs-work areas, daily streak status, XP earned with streak bonus, recommended next session based on weakness analysis. Weakness detection categorizes errors by mistake type: confusion pair, off-by-one, missing prerequisite, incorrect mental model, overgeneralization.
acceptanceCriteria: Session summary shown after practice session ends or user navigates away
Shows per-protocol star changes with level-up celebration
Accuracy breakdown by Bloom's level (remember/understand/apply/analyze/evaluate)
Top strength and top weakness identified with specific concept names
Recommended next session based on weakest areas
Mistake type classification for wrong answers

title: Add spaced repetition system — resurface weak concepts on expanding intervals
description: After practice sessions, schedule review questions: 1 day, 3 days, 7 days, 14 days. Wrong answers get shorter intervals; correct answers get longer. Mix questions from different protocols (interleaving). Research: University of Leicester found spaced repetition users scored 70% vs 64% for massed study. Hundreds of cognitive psychology studies confirm spacing produces superior long-term learning. Display upcoming reviews as a calendar with protocol indicators.
acceptanceCriteria: Review questions scheduled after each practice session based on performance
Intervals expand for correct answers: 1d→3d→7d→14d→30d
Intervals contract for wrong answers back to 1 day
Review sessions mix questions from multiple protocols (interleaving)
Calendar view shows upcoming reviews with protocol icons
Notification/prompt when reviews are due

title: Add 5 comparison challenges — analyze protocol trade-offs on same data
description: Test analytical thinking: (1) HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 under 1% packet loss — which degrades more gracefully? (2) REST vs GraphQL for a public API consumed by unknown third-party developers — which is safer? (3) TCP vs UDP for game state updates at 60fps — which handles loss better? (4) MQTT vs HTTP for IoT with 256KB RAM — which fits? (5) TLS 1.2 vs TLS 1.3 — which is better when middleboxes are present? Each presents the scenario, asks for analysis, then reveals detailed comparison.
acceptanceCriteria: 5 comparison challenges with structured analysis interface
Each presents two protocols side-by-side with a specific scenario
User must select winner AND explain their reasoning (checkbox of factors)
Detailed explanation shows all trade-offs with the visualization running both scenarios
Covers Bloom's Level 4 (Analyze) objectives
